chapter_info.subject: Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: The World of Metals and Non Metals

questions.question: What is metallic lustre?
questions.answer: The shiny, reflective appearance characteristic of metals.
questions.solution: Metallic lustre is the distinctive shiny or lustrous quality seen on metal surfaces. When light hits a metal surface, it reflects uniformly, creating the bright, reflective appearance we associate with metals. Copper, aluminium, and iron all exhibit this metallic lustre. Most non-metals lack this property—they appear dull or have a different type of appearance. The metallic lustre is one of the first observable differences distinguishing metals from non-metals.

questions.question: Define malleability.
questions.answer: The property by which materials can be beaten into thin sheets without breaking.
questions.solution: Malleability describes how certain materials respond to impact or pressure—they flatten and deform without fracturing. When copper, aluminium, or iron is struck with a hammer, it becomes flatter and thinner. This property is utilized in practical applications: silver foil on sweets, aluminium foil for food wrapping, and metal sheets for roofing. Most metals possess malleability. Non-metals like coal or sulfur are brittle instead—they break into pieces when hammered rather than flattening.

questions.question: What are the most malleable metals?
questions.answer: Gold and silver are the most malleable metals.
questions.solution: Gold and silver can be beaten into extremely thin sheets—so thin they are almost transparent in some cases. This exceptional malleability made them historically valuable for jewellery and decoration. The ability to form gold and silver into intricate shapes and thin ornaments relies directly on their superior malleability compared to other metals. These metals' malleable properties combined with their rarity and luster made them prized throughout history.

questions.question: What is brittleness?
questions.answer: The property of materials that break into pieces when beaten instead of flattening.
questions.solution: Brittle materials are hard but lack the ability to deform without breaking. When coal or sulfur is struck with a hammer, it shatters into many pieces rather than flattening into sheets. Brittleness is the opposite of malleability. Some materials like wood are neither malleable nor brittle—they dent or splinter but don't flatten into sheets and don't shatter. Understanding these three different responses to hammering (flattening, breaking, or denting) helps identify material types.

questions.question: Define ductility.
questions.answer: The property by which materials can be drawn into thin wires without breaking.
questions.solution: Ductility describes the ability of materials to be pulled or stretched into thin, continuous wires. Copper and aluminium wires used in electrical systems demonstrate ductility. This property is different from malleability—malleability involves compression (hammering), while ductility involves tension (pulling). A material can be both malleable and ductile (most metals are), but the properties represent different ways of deforming without breaking.

questions.question: Name three common uses of metal wires.
questions.answer: Electrical wiring, ornaments (bangles, necklaces, earrings), and musical instrument strings.
questions.solution: Metal wires are ubiquitous in modern life because their ductility allows them to be thin and flexible while maintaining strength. Electrical systems use copper and aluminium wires because they both conduct electricity and can be drawn into thin wires. Ornaments made from metal wires exploit both their appearance (luster) and their ductility. Musical instruments like veena, sitar, violin, and guitar use metal wires as strings because they produce clear tones and can withstand the tension required. The ductility of metals makes all these applications possible.

questions.question: What is sonority?
questions.answer: The property of metals to produce a ringing or musical sound when struck.
questions.solution: Sonority is the characteristic of metals to vibrate and produce clear, ringing sounds when struck or dropped. A metal spoon dropped on the floor produces a distinct ringing sound, while a piece of coal produces a dull thud. This property is directly observable and becomes the basis for making musical instruments and bells. School bells ring clearly because they are made of metal. The ringing sound of ghungroos (ankle bells worn in Indian dance) results from metal sonority. This property is not just useful for music—it can also be diagnostic for identifying whether an object is metal.

questions.question: Compare the sounds produced when metal and non-metal objects are dropped.
questions.answer: Metals produce distinct ringing sounds; non-metals produce dull, short-lived sounds.
questions.solution: When identical experiments are performed—dropping metal objects (spoon, coin) and non-metal objects (coal, wood) from the same height—the auditory differences are immediately apparent. Metal objects produce clear, resonant, ringing sounds that continue for a moment. Non-metal objects produce dull, thud-like sounds that end abruptly. This clear auditory difference makes sound an easy way to identify whether an object is metal. The ringing quality of metals relates to how their molecular structure vibrates when struck, producing sustained oscillations that create the ringing tone.

questions.question: Why are cooking vessels made of metal?
questions.answer: Because metals are good conductors of heat, efficiently transferring heat to food.
questions.solution: Cooking requires transferring heat from the heat source to the food being cooked. Metals are excellent heat conductors—they readily transfer heat from hot regions (bottom of vessel in contact with heat source) to cooler regions (interior where food is). This property makes them ideal for cooking vessels. Aluminium, copper, stainless steel, and iron are all used for cookware because of their thermal conductivity. Non-metals like ceramic or glass are poor heat conductors, which is why they are not suitable for cookware but may be used for other purposes.

questions.question: Why are handles of cooking vessels made of wood or plastic instead of metal?
questions.answer: Because wood and plastic are poor conductors of heat, keeping handles cool and safe to touch.
questions.solution: If cooking vessel handles were made of metal (good conductor), the heat from the vessel body would conduct up through the handle, making it too hot to touch. Wood and plastic are poor heat conductors—heat travels slowly through them. This means that even though the vessel interior is hot, the handle remains relatively cool. This design principle—using good heat conductors for the vessel body and poor heat conductors for handles—demonstrates practical application of understanding heat conduction. Safety and functionality depend directly on material properties.

questions.question: Define conduction of heat.
questions.answer: The transfer of heat from one point to another within a material or between materials in contact.
questions.solution: Conduction occurs when thermal energy (heat) moves through a material or from one object to another through direct contact, without the material moving. In Activity 4.3, when both metal and wooden spoons are immersed in hot water: Heat conducts through the metal spoon, making it hot all the way to the top. Heat conducts slowly through the wooden spoon, remaining cool at the top. Materials that conduct heat readily are called heat conductors (metals). Materials that conduct heat poorly are called insulators or poor conductors. Understanding conduction is essential for applications from cooking to building insulation.

questions.question: What is a good conductor of electricity?
questions.answer: A material that allows electricity to flow through it easily.
questions.solution: Good conductors of electricity are materials with properties that permit electric current to flow with minimal resistance. Metals like copper, aluminium, iron, and silver are excellent conductors. When such materials are placed in a circuit (as tested in Activity 4.4), they complete the circuit and allow the bulb to glow. Good electrical conductors are used for wiring and electrical components because they efficiently transmit electrical power. The effectiveness of electrical systems depends on using good conductors where electricity needs to flow.

questions.question: What is an insulator or poor conductor of electricity?
questions.answer: A material that prevents electricity from flowing through it.
questions.solution: Poor conductors of electricity (insulators) are materials that resist the flow of electric current. Sulfur, coal, wood, rubber, plastic, and nylon are poor conductors. When such materials are placed in an electrical circuit, they prevent current from flowing and the bulb does not glow. These materials are deliberately chosen for specific applications: Plastic insulation covers electrical wires to protect from shock. Rubber gloves and shoes protect electricians. Plastic handles on screwdrivers prevent electrical shock. In each case, the poor conducting property is desirable for safety.

questions.question: Why do electricians wear rubber gloves and shoes?
questions.answer: Rubber is a poor conductor, so it prevents electricity from flowing through the body if contact is made.
questions.solution: Electrical safety depends on understanding conductivity. If an electrician touches a live electrical wire with bare skin (good conductor), electricity flows through the body to the ground, causing shock. Rubber (poor conductor) blocks this flow: Rubber gloves prevent electricity from entering the hands. Rubber-soled shoes prevent electricity from flowing to the ground. The plastic handles on screwdrivers also provide insulation. Each safety measure is based directly on the principle that good conductors (like the human body) can be protected from electricity by surrounding them with poor conductors (rubber, plastic). This is life-saving application of material properties.

questions.question: What causes iron to rust?
questions.answer: Exposure to both water AND air (moist air) combined.
questions.solution: Rusting requires both components: water (moisture) and air (oxygen). Neither alone causes rust—this is the key finding from Activity 4.5. Bottle A: Dry air only → No rust. Bottle B: Water only (air excluded by oil) → No rust. Bottle C: Both water and air (moist conditions) → Rust develops. The chemical process requires moisture to facilitate the reaction between iron and atmospheric oxygen. Dry air alone, or water alone without air access, cannot produce rust. This understanding is crucial for prevention strategies.

questions.question: What is rust?
questions.answer: The brown deposit formed on iron when exposed to moist air (water and oxygen together).
questions.solution: Rust is a brown, crumbly compound formed when iron reacts chemically with oxygen in the presence of water. Chemically, rust is iron oxide combined with water. The appearance of rust indicates ongoing deterioration of the iron structure. Objects that rust become weaker and less functional. The faster rust develops in wet climates and coastal areas (more moisture and salt in air) than in dry regions. Understanding what rust is helps explain why prevention is important and why certain methods (painting, oiling, coating) work to stop rust formation.

questions.question: What is corrosion?
questions.answer: Gradual deterioration of metal surfaces caused by air, water, or other substances.
questions.solution: Corrosion is the general term for chemical deterioration of metals when exposed to environmental conditions. While rusting is specific to iron (formation of iron oxide), corrosion applies to all metals: Copper corrodes to develop green coating (copper oxide). Silver corrodes to develop black coating (silver oxide). Iron undergoes corrosion (called rusting). The rate and nature of corrosion depend on the metal type and the environmental conditions. All metals are susceptible to some form of corrosion, though the degree varies. This is why protective measures and material selection are important in applications requiring durability.

questions.question: Name three methods to prevent rusting of iron.
questions.answer: Painting, oiling/greasing, and galvanization (coating with zinc).
questions.solution: Since rusting requires both water and oxygen, prevention methods work by blocking one or both: Painting creates a barrier between iron and both water and air. Oiling or greasing also creates a protective layer preventing water and air contact. Galvanization involves coating iron with a layer of zinc metal that: (1) creates a physical barrier, (2) reacts with oxygen to form its own protective oxide layer, making the iron underneath safer. Waxing is another method using similar principles. Each method reflects understanding that preventing water-oxygen-iron contact stops rust formation. The choice of method depends on cost and application.

questions.question: What did the students learn from visiting an ironsmith?
questions.answer: That iron can be heated and beaten into different shapes, demonstrating its malleability.
questions.solution: The ironsmith explained his craft making items like pans, buckets, and tools from iron. When heated in a furnace, iron becomes pliable. The ironsmith then beats it with a hammer to shape it. This practical demonstration showed that iron has the property of malleability—it can be deformed by force without breaking. This observation prompted the key question that drives the chapter: What properties do metals have that allow them to be shaped this way? The answer involves all the metal properties studied: malleability, ductility, heat conductivity, etc.

questions.question: Why are only a few metals suitable for making jewellery?
questions.answer: Because jewellery requires materials that are lustrous, malleable, ductile, durable, and resistant to corrosion.
questions.solution: Not all metals make good jewellery. Ideal jewellery metals must have multiple properties: Lustrous appearance (aesthetically appealing), Malleability and ductility (can be crafted into intricate designs), Resistance to corrosion (maintain beauty over time), Sufficient hardness (resist scratching and damage). Gold, silver, and platinum are primarily used for jewellery because they excel in all these properties. Many other metals lack one or more of these characteristics: Iron rusts quickly, aluminium corrodes, copper tarnishes, and many are not lustrous enough or are too brittle. This demonstrates how material selection depends on understanding multiple property requirements for a specific application.

questions.question: Explain the results of Activity 4.5 (rusting of iron nails in three different conditions).
questions.answer: Bottle A (dry air): No rust. Bottle B (water only): No rust. Bottle C (moist air): Rust develops clearly.
questions.solution: This activity systematically tests the conditions necessary for rust formation: Bottle A contains dry air (silica gel removes moisture). The nail remains clean—demonstrating that dry air alone does not cause rusting. Bottle B contains water with oil layer preventing air contact. The nail remains clean—demonstrating that water alone (without oxygen) does not cause rusting. Bottle C has both water and air in contact with the nail. Brown deposits clearly develop—demonstrating that both water AND air are necessary. This experimental design isolates variables systematically. The clear results provide strong evidence that moist air (combination of water and oxygen) is the cause of rust. This understanding is crucial for design of rust prevention—simply keeping iron dry OR excluding air (but not necessarily both) can prevent rusting.

questions.question: Design an experiment to test whether sulfur conducts electricity.
questions.answer: Use an electrical tester circuit with sulfur sample inserted. Bulb won't glow indicating poor conduction.
questions.solution: Design: Create a simple electrical circuit (like the tester described in Activity 4.4). Procedure: Place a piece of sulfur across the circuit in the position where current needs to flow through it. Prediction: Based on the understanding that non-metals are poor electrical conductors, the bulb should not glow. Result: The sulfur blocks the electrical path—the bulb does not glow. Conclusion: Sulfur is a poor conductor of electricity, confirming it is a non-metal. This simple test can be used to distinguish metals from non-metals. The inability to conduct electricity is one characteristic that defines non-metals.

questions.question: Compare the properties of metals and non-metals.
questions.answer: Metals are lustrous, malleable, ductile, sonorous, high in hardness, good heat/electrical conductors. Non-metals are non-lustrous, non-malleable, non-ductile, non-sonorous, variable hardness, poor heat/electrical conductors.
questions.solution:
Making a detailed comparison clarifies the distinctions:

Lustre: Metals—lustrous (shiny). Non-metals—non-lustrous (dull).
Malleability: Metals—can be beaten into sheets. Non-metals—brittle, break when beaten.
Ductility: Metals—can be drawn into wires. Non-metals—cannot form wires.
Sonority: Metals—produce ringing sounds. Non-metals—produce dull sounds.
Hardness: Metals—generally hard (with exceptions like sodium). Non-metals—variable, usually soft or medium.
Heat Conduction: Metals—good conductors. Non-metals—poor conductors.
Electrical Conduction: Metals—good conductors. Non-metals—poor conductors.
Reactivity with Oxygen: Metals—form basic oxides. Non-metals—form acidic oxides.

This comprehensive comparison shows that metals have a consistent set of properties making them useful for specific applications, while non-metals have a very different set of properties making them useful for different applications.

questions.question: Why does copper develop a green coating while silver develops a black coating when exposed to air?
questions.answer: Different metals form different oxides with different colors when they react with atmospheric oxygen.
questions.solution: Copper exposed to air reacts with oxygen to form copper oxide, which has a green color (called verdigris). Silver exposed to air reacts with oxygen to form silver oxide, which has a black color (called tarnish). The specific color depends on the chemical identity of the oxide formed. Both processes are corrosion—the deterioration of metal surfaces due to environmental exposure. The different colors result from the different atomic structures and light-absorbing properties of different metal oxides. This demonstrates that while all metals undergo corrosion, the specific appearance of corrosion products varies. Understanding this difference helps in preservation of historical metal objects and choosing protective methods appropriate for specific metals.

questions.question: Explain Activity 4.6 results: Why does magnesium burn with a white flame?
questions.answer: Magnesium reacts vigorously with atmospheric oxygen, producing magnesium oxide (white powder) and releasing energy as heat and light.
questions.solution: When magnesium ribbon is heated, it ignites in air and undergoes this reaction: Magnesium + Oxygen → Magnesium Oxide. The reaction is highly exothermic (releases enormous energy). The visible white flame is radiation of this energy from the intensely hot reaction. The white powder produced is magnesium oxide. This reactive behavior contrasts with less reactive metals. The dazzling white light is so bright it's used in photography and displays. This demonstrates that some metals react vigorously with oxygen, showing the reactivity spectrum among metals. Such reactive metals are stored carefully to prevent unwanted reactions.

questions.question: Why is magnesium oxide basic in nature? (Testing with litmus paper)
questions.answer: Metal oxides are generally basic. Magnesium oxide solution turns red litmus blue, indicating basicity.
questions.solution: When magnesium oxide (white powder from Activity 4.6) is dissolved in water, it forms magnesium hydroxide solution. Using acid-base indicators: Red litmus paper turns blue in the solution—the defining characteristic of basic substances. This observation is part of a pattern: All metal oxides are generally basic in nature. This provides another way to distinguish metals from non-metals. Non-metal oxides, by contrast, are acidic in nature. This chemical difference reflects fundamental differences in atomic structure between metals and non-metals. The basicness of metal oxides becomes important in later studies of chemical reactions and acid-base chemistry.

questions.question: Explain Activity 4.7: What happens when sulfur burns and the product dissolves in water?
questions.answer: Sulfur burns to form sulfur dioxide gas. Dissolved in water, it forms sulfurous acid (acidic solution).
questions.solution: The reaction sequence: Sulfur (non-metal) + Oxygen (from air) → Sulfur Dioxide (pungent gas). When sulfur dioxide gas is dissolved in water: Sulfur Dioxide + Water → Sulfurous Acid. Testing with acid-base indicator: Blue litmus paper turns red in the solution—indicating acidity. This contrasts sharply with metal oxide behavior. While metal oxides are basic, non-metal oxides are acidic. This chemical difference is a key property distinguishing metals from non-metals at the molecular level. The acidic nature of non-metal oxide solutions is one indicator of whether a substance is metal or non-metal.

questions.question: Why must phosphorus be stored in water?
questions.answer: Phosphorus spontaneously catches fire when exposed to atmospheric oxygen at room temperature.
questions.solution: Phosphorus is an extremely reactive non-metal. When exposed to air, it reacts so vigorously with oxygen that it spontaneously ignites—without requiring external heat or spark. This property makes it dangerous to handle in normal conditions. Storing phosphorus in water prevents this reaction by excluding air access. The water acts as the barrier preventing oxygen contact. Despite being a non-metal with typical non-metal properties (poor conductor, brittle, dull, etc.), phosphorus shows extreme reactivity similar to reactive metals like sodium. This demonstrates that reactivity exists on a spectrum and some non-metals are more reactive than others. The storage requirement reflects understanding that preventing air contact prevents problems.

questions.question: Why is sodium stored in kerosene?
questions.answer: Sodium reacts vigorously with oxygen and water, so kerosene (oil) prevents air and moisture contact.
questions.solution: Sodium is a highly reactive metal—even more reactive than magnesium. It reacts vigorously with atmospheric oxygen, and even more vigorously with water, generating dangerous amounts of heat. Storing sodium in kerosene oil prevents both oxygen and moisture from contacting the metal. The kerosene creates a barrier that eliminates both reactants' access. This is a practical application of understanding how reactivity depends on material properties and environmental factors. The extreme reactivity of sodium contrasts with less reactive metals like aluminum or iron, demonstrating the reactivity spectrum among metals. Proper storage methods are critical for managing materials with extreme chemical reactivity.

questions.question: What is the difference between an element and a material?
questions.answer: Elements are pure substances (like copper, sulfur), while materials can be combinations (like plastic, wood, which are compounds/mixtures).
questions.solution: Distinction: Elements are pure substances consisting of atoms of a single type. They cannot be broken down into simpler substances. Examples: Copper (metal element), sulfur (non-metal element), oxygen (non-metal element). Materials are physical substances that may be elements, compounds, or mixtures. Examples: Plastic (compound made of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen), wood (mixture of cellulose, lignin, other compounds), glass (compound of silicon dioxide and other elements). Classification of 'metal' or 'non-metal' applies only to elements. Plastic, glass, rubber, and paper are not metals or non-metals because they are not pure elements. This distinction prevents confusion when discussing properties. Only elemental properties (like conductivity of copper metal) can be directly compared; material properties (like the hardness of composite wood) may not follow elemental patterns.

questions.question: How many elements are currently known?
questions.answer: 118 elements are known, including natural and artificially synthesized elements.
questions.solution: The current periodic table shows 118 confirmed elements. Some of these occur naturally in Earth (like copper, oxygen, nitrogen). Others are artificially synthesized in laboratories and do not exist naturally in significant quantities. New elements are occasionally discovered or synthesized, though most have been known for decades. These 118 elements are the fundamental building blocks of all matter in the universe. They combine in millions of different ways to form all substances we encounter. Understanding the periodic table and properties of elements is central to chemistry. The 118 elements are organized in patterns based on their properties—a fundamental framework for understanding structure of matter.

questions.question: Analyze this scenario: A metal object is painted, then placed in a humid environment. Will it rust? Explain your reasoning using understanding of rust formation.
questions.answer: No, it will not rust because paint creates a barrier preventing water and oxygen contact with the metal.
questions.solution: Reasoning from principles established in Activity 4.5: Rusting requires both water AND oxygen in contact with iron. The paint layer physically separates the iron from both water and atmospheric oxygen. Even though the environment is humid (water available) and contains oxygen (air present), these cannot reach the iron beneath the paint barrier. The paint maintains its protective function as long as it remains intact and doesn't chip or peel. If the paint becomes damaged, water and oxygen can enter through the break, initiating rust formation at that point. This demonstrates that understanding the requirements for rust formation (water AND oxygen together) allows us to predict outcomes of protection methods. The thickness and quality of paint affects the durability of protection.

questions.question: Compare the reactivities of sodium and magnesium with oxygen and water based on chemical observations from the chapter.
questions.answer: Sodium is more reactive than magnesium—it reacts vigorously with both oxygen and water at room temperature, while magnesium requires heating before reacting.
questions.solution: Evidence from the chapter: Magnesium requires heating (in a flame) before it reacts with oxygen to produce the dazzling white flame. Once ignited, the reaction is vigorous and exothermic. Sodium reacts vigorously with oxygen and water spontaneously at room temperature—without requiring external heat. It must be stored in kerosene to prevent contact with these reactants. This comparison reveals that: (1) Reactivity exists on a spectrum among metals, (2) Some metals react readily with oxygen, others require heating, (3) Water dramatically increases reactivity for some metals like sodium, (4) Storage and handling methods must match the degree of reactivity. This understanding applies to selecting appropriate storage and handling procedures for different materials based on their inherent reactivity.

questions.question: Design a comprehensive experiment to determine whether an unknown material is a metal or non-metal, using all the properties discussed in the chapter.
questions.answer: Test: appearance (luster), response to hammering (malleability), ability to form wires (ductility), sound when struck (sonority), heat conduction, electrical conduction, and oxide nature.
questions.solution:
Systematic Testing Protocol:

1. Appearance Test: Is it lustrous (shiny)? Metals: yes. Non-metals: no.

2. Malleability Test: Strike with hammer. Does it flatten into sheets? Metals: yes. Non-metals: break or show other response.

3. Ductility Test: Can it be drawn into wires? Metals: yes. Non-metals: no.

4. Sonority Test: Drop or strike. Does it produce ringing sound? Metals: yes. Non-metals: dull sound.

5. Heat Conduction Test: Immerse in hot water. Does handle become hot quickly? Metals: yes. Non-metals: no.

6. Electrical Conduction Test: Place in circuit. Does bulb glow? Metals: yes. Non-metals: no.

7. Oxide Nature Test: Burn the material (if safe), dissolve product in water, test with litmus paper. Result: Metal oxide→blue litmus stays blue (or becomes blue if red)—basic. Non-metal oxide→blue litmus turns red—acidic.

Interpretation: If most tests indicate metal properties, it's likely a metal. If results indicate non-metal properties, it's non-metal. Some materials might show mixed properties if they are alloys (metal combinations) or compounds (elements combined chemically). This systematic approach uses multiple characteristic properties to reliably identify whether a mystery material is metal or non-metal.

questions.question: The Iron Pillar of Delhi has remained rust-free for over 1600 years. What does this tell us about ancient Indian metallurgy and what methods might have been used?
questions.answer: Ancient Indians possessed sophisticated metallurgical knowledge about controlling corrosion through material composition and possible protective treatments.
questions.solution: The Iron Pillar's remarkable preservation despite 1600+ years of environmental exposure suggests: (1) The iron used had special composition—possibly containing elements that resist oxidation, (2) The surface was possibly treated with protective coating or was specially prepared, (3) The iron may have been alloyed with other metals to resist corrosion, (4) The manufacturing process incorporated knowledge about preventing rust development. This achievement demonstrates that ancient Indian scientists and craftspeople understood concepts we now explain through chemistry: that certain alloys resist corrosion better than pure iron, that surface treatments can prevent rust, that material science could be applied to create durable structures. The pillar is evidence that empirical knowledge about material properties preceded modern scientific understanding. This achievement in ancient times remained unmatched by Western metallurgy until modern corrosion science developed. Investigating how this ancient knowledge was obtained and transmitted provides insight into the sophisticated technological capabilities of ancient India.
